howItWorks.intro: This poster size calculator uses established formulas to provide accurate results.
rules: Max Print Size = Pixel Dimensions ÷ DPI
Print Cost = (Width × Height ÷ 144) × Price per Square Foot
howItWorks.outro: Results are estimates based on standard formulas. Verify with current local data for your specific situation.

faq.question: What resolution do I need for a poster?
faq.answer: It depends on viewing distance. Close-up posters need 200-300 DPI. Wall posters viewed from 3+ feet away look sharp at 100-150 DPI. Billboards can be as low as 15-30 DPI.

faq.question: What are standard poster sizes?
faq.answer: Common sizes: 11×17" (small), 18×24" (medium), 24×36" (large/movie poster), 27×40" (theatrical). A2 (16.5×23.4") and A1 (23.4×33.1") are international standards.

faq.question: How much does poster printing cost?
faq.answer: Basic poster printing costs $2-$8 per square foot. Photo-quality glossy or canvas prints run $5-$15/sq ft. Rush orders and mounting add to the cost.

faq.question: Is the Poster Size Calculator free to use?
faq.answer: Yes, completely free with no signup required. Use it as many times as you need — there are no limits or hidden fees.

faq.question: How accurate is this calculator?
faq.answer: This calculator uses standard practical calculation formulas trusted by users. Results are reliable estimates for planning purposes. For critical decisions, we recommend consulting a qualified professional to verify.

sections.title: What Is the Poster Size Calculator?
sections.body: The Poster Size Calculator is a free online tool designed for users who need quick, accurate calculations in the practical calculation space. By entering your image width, image height, viewing distance, you get instant results including max size at 300 dpi, max size at 150 dpi, recommended dpi. No formulas to memorize, no spreadsheets to build — just enter your numbers and get the answer in seconds. Whether you're a beginner or experienced professional, this calculator saves you time and eliminates guesswork.

sections.title: Why This Calculation Matters
sections.body: Getting max size at 300 dpi right can make the difference between success and costly mistakes. In practical calculation, small errors compound quickly. Manual calculations are error-prone and time-consuming, especially under pressure. This calculator applies proven formulas used by users worldwide, giving you confidence that your numbers are correct. Use it to get accurate results with precision and avoid common pitfalls that trip up beginners.

sections.title: When Should You Use This Calculator?
sections.body: This tool is most useful when you know your image width and need to find the right max size at 300 dpi. It's also great for quick estimates before committing to a decision, and to double-check manual calculations or professional quotes, and when comparing different scenarios side by side. Bookmark this page and come back whenever you need a fast, reliable answer — the calculator is always free and requires no signup.

howToSteps.title: Enter Your Image Width (px)

tips: Double-check your image width before calculating — even small input errors can significantly change your results.
